name: Timmy C
subtitle: Push A / Pull A / Legs A / Push B / Pull B / Legs B

days.label: Push A
days.title: Push A — Chest, Shoulders & Triceps
days.subtitle: Heavy bench, dumbbell pressing, cable isolation, tricep finishers.

exercises.name: Barbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Strength focus; wide rep range — build to a heavy top set
exercises.tip: Eyes under bar, slight arch, shoulder blades pinched. Forearms vertical at the bottom. Elbows 45-75° from torso — not flared to 90°. Half-second pause at chest eliminates momentum. With a 3-10 range, consider working up to a heavy set of 3 then back-off sets.
formTips: Retract and depress shoulder blades before unracking
Wrists straight — bar sits on heel of palm
Drive feet into floor for leg drive
Bar path: slight diagonal from chest to over shoulders
commonMistakes: Bouncing bar off chest
Flaring elbows to 90 degrees
Lifting hips off bench

exercises.name: Dumbbell Shoulder Press
exercises.notes: Heavy DB pressing; control the negative
exercises.tip: Seated with bench at 80-85° (not fully vertical). Lower dumbbells to ear level minimum every rep — the stretched position at the bottom is where growth stimulus is highest. Press up and slightly inward. Don't bounce at the bottom.
formTips: Set bench to 80-85°, not fully vertical
Lower dumbbells to at least ear level every rep
Press up and slightly inward
commonMistakes: Bench set too upright (turns into a trap exercise)
Not lowering deep enough — cutting ROM
Arching lower back excessively

exercises.name: Weighted Triceps Dip
exercises.notes: Add belt weight; upright torso for triceps focus
exercises.tip: To target triceps over chest: keep torso upright (not hunched forward), elbows close to your sides, and lower just until elbows hit 90°. Going deeper shifts load to the chest. Add a plate via dip belt once bodyweight becomes easy.
formTips: Keep torso upright for triceps focus
Elbows close to your sides throughout
Lower to 90° elbow angle — don't go deeper for triceps focus
commonMistakes: Leaning too far forward (shifts load to chest)
Flaring elbows out wide
Not achieving full lockout at the top

exercises.name: Cable Fly Crossover
exercises.notes: Mid-chest; 1 sec squeeze at peak
exercises.tip: Set pulleys at chest height. Lock your elbow angle throughout (slight bend). Try crossing hands slightly at peak for extra inner chest activation. Control the eccentric — don't let the cables yank your arms back.
formTips: Pulleys at chest height
Lock slight bend in elbows throughout
Cross hands slightly at peak for extra squeeze
commonMistakes: Bending elbows too much (turns into a press)
Letting cables yank arms back uncontrolled
Using too much weight and losing form

exercises.name: Low Cable Fly Crossover
exercises.notes: Upper chest emphasis; pulleys at lowest position
exercises.tip: Pulleys at lowest position. Sweep arms up in an arc to chin height. Cables provide constant tension through the entire range — squeeze hard at peak contraction. Slight forward lean. This hits the upper/inner chest that flat pressing misses.
formTips: Pulleys at lowest position
Sweep arms in an arc up to chin height
Squeeze hard at peak contraction
commonMistakes: Bending elbows too much (turns into a press)
Using too much weight and losing the arc
Not bringing hands high enough

exercises.name: Triceps Rope Pushdown
exercises.notes: Spread rope at bottom for lateral head
exercises.tip: Split the rope apart at the bottom and hold for a beat — this targets the lateral head which gives arm width (the horseshoe shape). Lock elbows at your sides throughout. If your elbows drift forward, the weight is too heavy.
formTips: Lock elbows at your sides throughout
Split rope apart at full extension and squeeze
Controlled return — don't let the stack slam
commonMistakes: Elbows drifting forward (too much weight)
Not splitting the rope at the bottom
Using body momentum to push down

exercises.name: Dumbbell Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Lead with pinkies; strict form
exercises.tip: Slight 5-10° forward lean to better isolate the lateral (side) delt. Stop at shoulder height — going higher recruits traps. Imagine pouring water from a pitcher. Strict form always beats heavy weight on this exercise.
formTips: Slight 5-10° forward lean
Stop at shoulder height — no higher
Lead with pinkies as if pouring water
commonMistakes: Going above shoulder height (recruits traps)
Using momentum to swing the weight
Going too heavy and losing strict form

days.label: Pull A
days.title: Pull A — Back & Biceps
days.subtitle: Heavy deadlift, weighted chin-ups, rowing, shrugs, bicep finishers.

exercises.name: Barbell Deadlift
exercises.notes: Strength focus; reset fully between reps
exercises.tip: Stand mid-foot under bar. Hip hinge to grip — bar over mid-foot, hips above knees. Big breath and brace before you pull. Push the floor away (leg drive), then lock hips through at the top. Re-set every rep — this is not a touch-and-go exercise.
formTips: Bar over mid-foot, hips above knees
Full brace before each rep — reset stance
Push the floor away, then drive hips through
Bar stays in contact with shins and thighs
commonMistakes: Bar drifting away from the body
Rounding lower back under load
Jerking the bar off the floor

exercises.name: Weighted Chin-Up
exercises.notes: Supinated grip; dead hang each rep
exercises.tip: Supinated (underhand) grip puts biceps in a mechanically advantaged position — you'll feel them working significantly more than pull-ups. Start from a full dead hang every rep. Add a plate via dip belt once you hit 3×7 comfortably.
formTips: Supinated (underhand) grip
Full dead hang at the bottom every rep
Pull chin over the bar, control the descent
commonMistakes: Partial reps — not going to full dead hang
Kipping or swinging for momentum
Not getting chin over the bar

exercises.name: Seated Row (Machine)
exercises.notes: Squeeze shoulder blades at peak
exercises.tip: Pull with your elbows, not your hands — imagine hands are hooks. Let the weight stretch you forward slightly at the bottom (protract shoulders). Then squeeze shoulder blades together hard for a full second at peak contraction. This builds back thickness.
formTips: Pull with elbows, not hands — hands are hooks
Let shoulders protract fully at the stretch
Squeeze shoulder blades together for 1 second at peak
commonMistakes: Using momentum to swing the weight
Shrugging shoulders up instead of pulling back
Cutting range of motion short at the stretch

exercises.name: Dumbbell Shrug
exercises.notes: Hold at top 2 sec; no shoulder rolling
exercises.tip: Shrug straight up — do not roll your shoulders. Rolling can irritate the AC joint. Pause and hold at the top for 2 seconds to maximally contract the upper traps. Squeeze as if you're trying to touch your ears with your shoulders.
formTips: Shrug straight up — no shoulder rolling
Hold at the top for 2 full seconds
Full depression at the bottom between reps
commonMistakes: Rolling shoulders (AC joint injury risk)
Not pausing at the top
Using too much weight and losing range of motion

exercises.name: Barbell Bicep Curl
exercises.notes: Pin elbows; no swinging
exercises.tip: Pin your elbows to your sides — they're nailed to the wall. If they drift forward, you're turning this into a front raise. Curl through the full range and squeeze at the top. EZ-bar is an acceptable alternative if straight bar causes wrist discomfort.
formTips: Pin elbows to your sides throughout
Full range of motion — full extension at bottom
Squeeze at the top for 1 second
commonMistakes: Elbows drifting forward
Swinging the bar with body momentum
Cutting range of motion at the bottom

exercises.name: Rear Delt Reverse Fly (Machine)
exercises.notes: Elbows slightly bent; rear delt squeeze at peak
exercises.tip: Set the pads to chest height. Slight bend in elbows, drive elbows back and out in a wide arc. Squeeze rear delts at peak contraction — you should feel it in the back of your shoulders, not your traps. Lighter weight with better control beats heavy and sloppy.
formTips: Set pads at chest height
Drive elbows back in a wide arc
Squeeze rear delts — not traps — at peak
commonMistakes: Using traps instead of rear delts
Going too heavy and using momentum
Not getting full range of motion

days.label: Legs A
days.title: Legs A — Quads, Hamstrings & Calves
days.subtitle: Heavy squat, barbell lunges, machine volume, calf and hamstring finishers.

exercises.name: Barbell Squat
exercises.notes: Strength focus; hip crease below knee
exercises.tip: Hip crease below knee every rep. Big belly breath into your belt (or brace hard), push knees out over toes. Full-depth squats recruit significantly more muscle fiber than partials. Film yourself from the side to check depth until it's automatic.
formTips: Brace core before descent — belly breath into belt
Feet shoulder-width, toes out 15-30°
Knees track over toes, push out slightly
Control descent, explode up
commonMistakes: Knees caving inward
Rising hips first (good morning squat)
Not hitting proper depth

exercises.name: Barbell Lunge
exercises.notes: Alternating legs; front shin vertical
exercises.tip: Step far enough forward so your front shin stays vertical throughout. Back knee descends toward the floor without touching. Drive through the front heel to stand. Keep the barbell stable — don't let it tip. Alternating lunges build unilateral strength and address imbalances.
formTips: Front shin vertical at the bottom
Back knee descends close to the floor
Drive through the front heel to stand
commonMistakes: Front shin not vertical (knee shoots forward)
Leaning forward with the torso
Bar tipping due to instability

exercises.name: Leg Press (Machine)
exercises.notes: Full ROM; don't lock out knees
exercises.tip: Don't lock out your knees at the top — keep a slight bend. Lower until your knees are at or just past 90°. Foot placement matters: higher feet = more hamstrings/glutes, lower feet = more quads. Maintain contact with the back pad throughout.
formTips: Keep a slight knee bend at the top — don't lock out
Lower until knees reach 90° or just past
Back flat against the pad throughout
commonMistakes: Locking out knees at the top
Not hitting full depth
Lower back lifting off the pad

exercises.name: Calf Press (Machine)
exercises.notes: Full ROM; pause at stretch
exercises.tip: Full range of motion is essential for calves. Drop heels as low as possible for a deep stretch, then rise as high as possible. Pause 1-2 seconds at the bottom stretch — calves respond very well to the stretched position. Knees straight targets the gastrocnemius (the larger upper calf muscle).
formTips: Full range: drop heels low, rise as high as possible
Pause 1-2 seconds at the bottom stretch
Keep knees straight to target gastrocnemius
commonMistakes: Bouncing through reps without pausing
Cutting range of motion short
Going too heavy and losing control

exercises.name: Seated Leg Curl (Machine)
exercises.notes: Slow 3 sec eccentric; full range
exercises.tip: The seated leg curl keeps your hip flexed, which puts the hamstrings in a longer (more stretched) position than a lying leg curl. This makes it particularly effective for the proximal hamstring. Slow 3-second lowering phase is where most of the growth stimulus comes from. Curl as far as possible.
formTips: Lower over 3 full seconds every rep
Curl as far as possible toward your glutes
Pause at peak contraction before lowering
commonMistakes: Rushing the eccentric phase
Lifting hips off the pad
Not curling through full range of motion

days.label: Push B
days.title: Push B — Shoulders, Chest & Triceps
days.subtitle: Shoulder-led push session. Incline pressing. Machine chest isolation. Tricep volume.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Shoulder Press
exercises.notes: Lead exercise this session; 5 sets for shoulder volume
exercises.tip: Seated with bench at 80-85°. Lower dumbbells to ear level minimum every rep. On Push B this is your primary compound — 5 sets vs 3 in Push A. This increased volume drives shoulder hypertrophy. Press up and slightly inward. Don't bounce at the bottom.
formTips: Set bench to 80-85°, not fully vertical
Lower dumbbells to at least ear level every rep
Press up and slightly inward
commonMistakes: Bench set too upright (turns into a trap exercise)
Not lowering deep enough — cutting ROM
Arching lower back excessively

exercises.name: Incline Dumbbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: 30° incline; upper chest emphasis
exercises.tip: Set bench to 30° (one notch up on most benches). Lower dumbbells as deep as your mobility allows — the stretch at the bottom is the primary growth driver with dumbbells. The stretch position at the bottom of incline DB press is uniquely valuable for upper chest hypertrophy.
formTips: Set bench to 30° for optimal upper chest
Lower dumbbells as deep as mobility allows
Press up and slightly inward
commonMistakes: Bench angle too steep (becomes a shoulder press)
Not lowering deep enough for full stretch
Flaring elbows to 90°

exercises.name: Close Grip Bench Press
exercises.notes: Shoulder-width grip; tricep emphasis
exercises.tip: Use a shoulder-width grip (not uncomfortably narrow). This shifts load from chest to triceps while still working the chest secondarily. Elbows stay closer to your body. Lower the bar to lower chest and press straight up. Great for building the lateral and medial tricep heads.
formTips: Shoulder-width grip — not overly narrow
Elbows stay close to your sides
Lower to lower chest, press straight up
commonMistakes: Grip too narrow (causes wrist pain)
Elbows flaring out (becomes a regular bench press)
Not achieving full range of motion

exercises.name: Chest Fly (Machine)
exercises.notes: Full arc; 1 sec squeeze at peak
exercises.tip: The machine pec deck provides consistent resistance through the full arc. Maintain a slight bend in your elbows and don't let them go behind the pads. Focus on squeezing your chest at peak contraction — hold for 1 second. Full stretch on every rep.
formTips: Maintain slight bend in elbows throughout
Elbows don't go behind the pads
Squeeze chest hard for 1 second at peak
commonMistakes: Elbows going too far back (shoulder injury risk)
Not squeezing at the peak
Rushing through the eccentric

exercises.name: Triceps Pressdown
exercises.notes: Bar, rope or V-bar; full lockout
exercises.tip: Lock your elbows at your sides throughout. Use whichever attachment feels best — straight bar, V-bar, or rope. Achieve full lockout at the bottom of every rep to maximally contract the triceps. Control the ascent so you don't use shoulder momentum.
formTips: Lock elbows at your sides throughout
Full lockout at the bottom
Controlled ascent — no shoulder momentum
commonMistakes: Elbows drifting forward (too much weight)
Not achieving full lockout
Using body momentum to push down

exercises.name: Dumbbell Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Second lateral raise session; side delt volume
exercises.tip: Second lateral raise session of the week — side delts recover quickly and benefit from high frequency. Slight 5-10° forward lean, stop at shoulder height, lead with pinkies. Consider a slow eccentric or drop set on the last set for extra stimulus.
formTips: Slight 5-10° forward lean
Stop at shoulder height — no higher
Lead with pinkies as if pouring water
commonMistakes: Going above shoulder height (recruits traps)
Using momentum to swing the weight
Going too heavy and losing strict form

days.label: Pull B
days.title: Pull B — Back & Biceps
days.subtitle: Zercher deadlift, bent-over rows, weighted pull-ups, DB rows, bicep finishers.

exercises.name: Zercher Deadlift
exercises.notes: Bar cradled in elbow crease; extremely upright torso
exercises.tip: The Zercher deadlift is held in the crook of your elbows. This unique position forces an extremely upright torso and places high demand on the upper back, traps, and core. Start lighter than you think — the elbow position is uncomfortable at first. Wear long sleeves or use padding on the bar.
formTips: Bar rests in the crook of your elbows
Stay as upright as possible throughout
Brace hard — this variation is very core-demanding
commonMistakes: Leaning too far forward (loses the Zercher benefit)
Going too heavy before learning the pattern
Not bracing the core adequately

exercises.name: Bent Over Barbell Row
exercises.notes: Torso ~45°; pull to lower chest
exercises.tip: Hinge to roughly 45° torso angle. Bar hangs at arm's length. Pull to your lower chest/upper navel — not upper chest or belly. Think elbows back, not hands up. Shoulders stay retracted — don't let them shrug. Overhand grip hits more upper back; underhand hits more biceps.
formTips: Hinge to 45° torso angle and hold it
Pull bar to lower chest/upper navel
Think elbows back, not hands up
commonMistakes: Using body momentum to row the weight
Shrugging shoulders (traps dominant)
Standing too upright (turns into an upright row)

exercises.name: Weighted Pull-Up
exercises.notes: Overhand grip; dead hang each rep
exercises.tip: Overhand (pronated) grip targets the lats more than chin-ups. Start from a full dead hang every rep. Pull your chest toward the bar. Add a plate via dip belt once you hit 3×6 comfortably. This is the compound that builds the V-taper — treat it as a strength lift.
formTips: Full dead hang at the bottom every rep
Pull chest toward the bar, not just chin over
Control the descent — 2 full seconds down
commonMistakes: Partial reps — not going to full dead hang
Kipping or swinging
Only pulling chin over bar (not fully engaging lats)

exercises.name: Dumbbell Row
exercises.notes: Support on bench; drive elbow toward ceiling
exercises.tip: Support knee and hand on a bench. Let the dumbbell hang at full arm extension between reps. Row the elbow up and back — imagine driving it toward the ceiling. Think elbows, not hands. At the top, squeeze your lats and upper back. Allow slight torso rotation on heavy sets.
formTips: Full arm extension at the bottom every rep
Drive the elbow toward the ceiling
Squeeze at the top and hold 1 second
commonMistakes: Using biceps more than back
Not getting full stretch at the bottom
Using too much body swing

exercises.name: Seated Incline Dumbbell Curl
exercises.notes: Long head stretch; arms behind torso
exercises.tip: Set bench to 45-60°. Let arms hang behind your torso — keep elbows pinned behind your body throughout. This is one of the only exercises that targets the bicep long head (the peak) in its stretched position. Slow 3-second lowering. Go heavier than you'd expect — it's a relatively low-rep range.
formTips: Set bench to 45-60°, arms hang behind torso
Keep elbows pinned behind your body throughout
Slow 3-second lowering for maximum stretch
commonMistakes: Elbows drifting forward (loses the stretch)
Using momentum to swing the weight
Bench angle too upright (defeats the purpose)

exercises.name: Rear Delt Reverse Fly (Machine)
exercises.notes: Second rear delt session; controlled reps
exercises.tip: Second rear delt session this week. Drive elbows back and out in a wide arc. Squeeze rear delts at peak contraction — you should feel it in the back of your shoulders, not your traps. Use a weight that allows strict form across all 5 sets.
formTips: Set pads at chest height
Drive elbows back in a wide arc
Squeeze rear delts — not traps — at peak
commonMistakes: Using traps instead of rear delts
Going too heavy and using momentum
Not getting full range of motion

days.label: Legs B
days.title: Legs B — Full Lower Body
days.subtitle: Heavy squat, RDL, hip thrusts, lunges, machine isolation, core finisher.

exercises.name: Barbell Squat
exercises.notes: Heavier/lower rep than Legs A; max strength focus
exercises.tip: Legs B squat is 6 sets with a lower rep range — treat this as a strength session. Hip crease below knee every rep. Full brace, knees out, explode up. Consider working to a top set and doing back-off sets. Film yourself — depth is non-negotiable.
formTips: Brace core before descent — belly breath into belt
Feet shoulder-width, toes out 15-30°
Knees track over toes, push out slightly
Control descent, explode up
commonMistakes: Knees caving inward
Rising hips first (good morning squat)
Not hitting proper depth

exercises.name: Romanian Deadlift (Barbell)
exercises.notes: Hinge at hips; bar on legs; hamstring stretch
exercises.tip: Start from rack. Bar drags thighs throughout. 'Hips back' not 'bar down.' Stop when you feel a deep hamstring stretch. Keep knees slightly bent. Slow 3-second lowering amplifies the hamstring stretch and is where most of the growth stimulus occurs.
formTips: Bar drags along your thighs the entire way down
Push hips back — don't just bend forward
Maintain neutral spine — 'proud chest' throughout
Stop when you feel a deep hamstring stretch
commonMistakes: Rounding the lower back
Bar drifting away from the body
Bending knees too much (turns into a squat)

exercises.name: Barbell Hip Thrust
exercises.notes: Drive through heels; squeeze glutes at lockout
exercises.tip: Upper back on bench, bar over hip crease with pad. Drive through your heels. At the top, squeeze your glutes hard and hold — full hip extension is where glute activation peaks. Don't hyperextend your lower back; tuck pelvis slightly at lockout (posterior pelvic tilt). Control the descent.
formTips: Drive through heels, not toes
Squeeze glutes hard at full lockout and hold
Tuck pelvis slightly at the top (posterior pelvic tilt)
commonMistakes: Hyperextending lower back at lockout
Not achieving full hip extension
Pushing through toes instead of heels

exercises.name: Dumbbell Lunge
exercises.notes: Alternating or walking; front shin vertical
exercises.tip: Step far enough forward so your front shin is vertical throughout. This keeps tension on the quads without overloading the knee. Drive through the front heel to stand. After a heavy squat session, this is high-volume unilateral burnout — embrace the burn.
formTips: Front shin vertical at the bottom
Back knee descends close to the floor
Drive through the front heel to stand
commonMistakes: Front shin not vertical (knee shoots forward)
Leaning forward with the torso
Taking steps that are too short

exercises.name: Seated Calf Raise
exercises.notes: Seated targets soleus; full ROM
exercises.tip: Seated calf raises (knees bent) target the soleus — the deeper, wider calf muscle under the gastrocnemius. While standing raises target the gastroc, seated raises hit the soleus which is often underdeveloped. Full range of motion, pause at the bottom for a full stretch every rep.
formTips: Knees bent at 90° throughout
Full range: deep stretch at bottom, fully plantar-flex at top
Pause 1-2 seconds at the bottom stretch
commonMistakes: Bouncing through reps without pausing
Cutting range of motion short
Too much weight and losing control

exercises.name: Leg Extension (Machine)
exercises.notes: Full lockout; squeeze 1 sec at top
exercises.tip: Full lockout at the top, squeeze your quads hard for 1 second every rep — the peak contraction is uniquely valuable on this exercise. Align your knees with the machine's pivot point. Don't let the weight stack slam. After a heavy squat session this is pure quad isolation to cap off leg volume.
formTips: Full lockout at the top, squeeze quads 1 second
Align knees with the machine pivot point
Controlled lowering — don't let the stack slam
commonMistakes: Not fully extending the legs at the top
Using momentum to swing the weight
Knees misaligned with machine pivot

exercises.name: Hanging Leg Raise
exercises.notes: Curl pelvis up; control the descent
exercises.tip: Hang from a pull-up bar. The key is pelvic tilt at the top — curl your belt buckle toward your chin. Without this, hip flexors do all the work and abs barely engage. If you can't yet do straight-leg raises, start with bent-knee raises and progress. Add ankle weights to progress over time.
formTips: Curl pelvis up at the top — belt buckle to chin
Control the descent — no swinging
Keep upper body still throughout
commonMistakes: Lifting legs without pelvic tilt (hip flexors dominate)
Swinging and using momentum
Dropping legs uncontrolled
